blocks.content: By the last week of August, summer in Millhaven started to smell different. Still hot, still thick — but underneath the honeysuckle and the cut grass, something else came in.
School started in nine days. Kit had been not-thinking about this for two weeks.
He showed up at Daisy's door at noon. Mrs. Jones answered and looked at him the way she always did.

blocks.speaker: Mrs. Jones
blocks.content: She's in the kitchen. Go on through.
Daisy was at the kitchen table eating cereal, her hair still damp from a shower. She looked up when he came in.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: You ate?

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: Not yet.
She pushed the cereal box across the table without being asked. He got a bowl from the cabinet he already knew and sat down across from her.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: Eli called. He wants Hester's for lunch.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: Of course he does.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: Savannah's in. They're meeting us at noon-thirty.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: Noon-thirty isn't a time.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: Eli's been saying noon-thirty since fourth grade and we've never corrected him, so at this point it's basically a time.
Kit ate his cereal. Nine days.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: What are you reading?
She held up the cover. He didn't recognize it.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: Any good?

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: Really good.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: What's it about?

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: People not saying what they mean.
He looked at her. She looked at the book. The kitchen was quiet.
